column16: Some main ideas that I'd like to implement as Subject Director is to boost engagement. Possible engagement could be more practice tests where teams could work together. In Experimental Design meetings, we occasionally have interactive lessons such as Kahoots, in order to gauge general understand of certain topics.

column15:
I have been a member of Science Olympiad since sixth grade and served as Director of Biology (as well as co-captain) in seventh grade. Over the years, consistently competing in similar events—especially Anatomy & Physiology—has not only helped me develop a deep understanding of the material but also allowed me to master the most effective study techniques and resources. Holding a leadership position in middle school and ultimately leading my team to States—where my partner and I placed first in A&P—was one of the most rewarding experiences of my academic journey.

I have competed in the Boyceville invitational, BirdSo Satellite invitational (11th place A&P), Stuyvesant Invitational (8th place Disease Detectives), SealSO (6th in A&P), and of course most importantly last year I won three medals in the intra team competition :) Although I didn’t attend states this year I am eager to attend more in person tournaments next year.
column16:
One of my experiences with Biology events and Science Olympiad in general is that while the lessons provide a valuable baseline understanding of the material, it's impossible to cover everything within weekly 40-minute periods. To address this, as subject director, I would develop a comprehensive study plan for weekends or after-school sessions. This plan would include everyone watching a few selected videos each week. I’ve found that Armando Hasudungan—a YouTuber who creates hundreds of videos for pre-med students—effectively covers much of the content needed for Anatomy & Physiology, and I often use his resources myself. 

I also believe there should be greater transparency regarding cheat sheets. If every new member has to build theirs from scratch, it is very hard for newcomers to achieve competitive success. In addition to each person's individual cheat sheet, I propose having a shared team cheat sheet where members can contribute information and leave comments about any gaps or missing topics.
column17: As co-captain and head of biology events on my middle school’s Science Olympiad team, I coordinated events and tutored members—an experience that taught me how to balance academic responsibilities with leadership duties like making slideshows and editing notes. I'm also head of Art for the Journal of Biology, which reflects my broader engagement with science beyond competition. As mentioned before, if selected, I plan to support members outside of our weekly 40-minute sessions by creating shared resources and study tools. My teaching style mirrors how I study: I break down complex material into manageable sections and emphasize regular practice with realistic questions. I know from experience—especially when I started Disease Detectives in middle school—that the sheer volume of material can be overwhelming. That’s why I believe a strong teacher should not only be knowledgeable, but also supportive and encouraging.
column18: Although I’ve competed in many tournaments, I feel that over the past two years, I haven’t fully had the chance to demonstrate my commitment to the team or my potential as a leader. Science Olympiad is my primary extracurricular, and I’m eager to take on a role where I can contribute to decision-making and help improve how we study and prepare. I believe my experience equips me to make meaningful changes that benefit the whole team. Since joining SciOly in freshman year, I’ve been eager to get more involved, and this past year, I’ve made meaningful connections and friendships beyond my own events. At the end of the day, my biggest motivation for being a part of the science olympiad is that I am a nerd who loves learning and I want to teach and learn from more people and be in a position where I am available to help more people.

column16:
1. Improve current shared resources - a "General Concepts" doc covering fundamentals applicable to all events of my subject that all EMs will contribute to: lab-related skills, basic scientific principles, etc. For specific events, EMs don't have to repeat the concepts covered in the "General Concepts" doc and can put more in-depth materials relevant to their events, reducing repetitive work and facilitating collaboration among different events.

2. EM lesson tracker - let EMs put their intended lesson (very briefly), which should first accommodate "general" principles and then specialized materials. This encourages EMs to teach the fundamentals and then dive deeper into their own lessons, ensuring that the students with no prior experience are covered.

3. Survey - allow students to rate their experiences at their events. If major dissatisfaction is spotted for an event, the EM of the relevant event can be notified to find and resolve the concern.

column16:
Subject Problems of the Week: 3 of them, for physics there would be an easy/medium/hard. you can pull past scioly test problems, but also f=ma, usapho, or quizbowl.

Binder Review Sessions: This is a big one. A lot of people often just chatgpt or copy info into the binder without care for organization or coherency, and it can be overwhelming to only navigate the first time on competition day. We should have 1-2 lessons after binders are done specifically to go over everything.

Mandated Phet Simulator usage in lessons: Use Phet constantly to emphasize conceptual understanding over just mindless equation memorization.

Make BUILD components for academic events actually be in school: normally EMs just handle this part themselves without input from the rest of the team, leaving the design at high risk of being faulty (no outside input). Everyone should contribute to build over time, not 2 people cramming the week before.

CAD/CFD/FEA workshop-Teach Cad in Fusion 360, Solidworks, Autocad, but not JUST that-- go a step further, and teach CFD/FEA, which is basically a way to create real world simulations to model things like stress, strain, and fluid flow/resistance around your CAD design. you can edit and modify the differential equations that model these phenomena, which would be incredible pracice to have members apply something conceptual (calculations) to a real world problem.
column17:
EXTERNAL LEADERSHIP
-Cofounder/Head of Software Development @ Bxsciweb, a webdev initiative to improve student experience through smart learning/tech approach. Organized my department's meetings, in charge of development of various and select elements of the site (counselor, NHS, etc)
-Makerspace Treasurer-Raised 500+ dollars for 3d printers and other materials
-Chess Club President-Organized Meetings, made puzzles of the week, coordinated with NYC nonprofit leader to organize interschool tournaments
-Synagogue Youth Leader-Organize Religious Services for U18 attendees.

As a leader, specifically for STEM related roles, my holy mantra is to prioritize conceptual understanding over memorization. Often SciOly and leaders in other STEM teams undervalue the importance of understanding: most equations aren't just numbers relating variables, they are making statements about the real world, and using engaging and visual tools like Phet, Labster, and other resources makes the process of learning significantly more enriching and engaging for students.

TLDR--Instead of prioritizing memorization and individual work to throw team members at diagnostics and see what sticks, I prioritize teamwork and conceptual understanding whenever I teach (through visuals and hands on activities).
column18:
I want this position on the SciOly team because Science Olympiad is the first place in Bronx Science where I truly felt at home. My freshman year was filled with uncertainty and strain as I was adjusting, (especially with Honors Biology). 

         The SciOly team to me was the first thing I joined in this school, and it was the first place where it felt that the community were my type of people--curious, passionate, and dedicated individuals wanting to delve far deeper into science than anything that a class would require of them. No one forced SciOly members to join or pursue STEM to this degree, and yet every single person signed up for it out of nothing less than sheer drive and passion, which is precisely the kind of community that uplifts me and inspires me to push further in my pursuits.

There is a misconception that exists that says that SciOly is just competitive test taking, and not much else. But when I see Scioly, I see so much more than that---I see a team of students working together to acheive a common goal; I see people willing to stay up all night just to finish and perfect their binders and research further; I see a beautiful and exemplary opportunity for both academic and personal growth, because SciOly is more than just a team, its a family. 

The long hours and sleepless nights are precisely what makes SciOly special, because there is nothing more rewarding than sacrificing sleep for the sake of knowledge and problem solving surrounding by your friends.

column16:
When I first joined the team, I expected frequent communication between members of the same event/subject. To my surprise, I was only met with scattered email chains. Thus, I would implement the simple but necessary idea of groupchats, both among events and for the subject as a whole. This would mitigate the need for individual messages to peers and event managers, allowing for better practice for competitions and stronger relationships between members. 

Moreover, my peers and I have often found ourselves messaging busy event managers in the hopes of getting a hold of necessary files before competition days. To prevent this, I would maintain a spreadsheet of all binders and sheets made by members, which would allow both tenured event members and those newly competing in the event to access any and every file that they might need for preparation and competition day.

column15:
Although all events in this subject are new, I have significant hands-on experience with the topics they cover. As a Construction member of the 2-year FTC Robotics team, I helped them qualify for City Championships, placing in top 10. This hands-on experience in mechanical design and electrical systems directly aligns with the Engineering CAD and Circuit Lab events. I also have a 97.3% in AP Physics 1 and scored 11/25 on the F=ma exam (the cutoff for USAPhO was 14). My strong physics background allows me to effectively teach the Machines event, which mainly covers AP Physics 1 material.
column16: Unlike the past EMs’ lessons using a single practice sheet or brief summaries of incredibly complicated topics, I want to focus on more hands-on lessons and gradual build-ups to difficult concepts, allowing everyone to fully understand the material before major competitions. For example, in Engineering, we'll start with manipulating objects on Tinkercad (extrusion, making holes…) and slowly work our way up to more complex systems on Onshape (gear ratios, threading, etc.). In Machines, we'll begin with basic mechanical advantage concepts at a Regents level before tackling cumulative topics like Rotational Motion at an AP level. For Circuit Lab, we'll start with simple circuits (Ohm’s Law, Series and Parallel) and gradually introduce more complex components (motors, capacitors, etc.). I’ll use breadboards to try and teach these concepts as efficiently as possible. All resources and practice problems will be posted on Google Classroom at the beginning of the year, allowing students to progress at their own pace and revisit materials if needed.
column17: I'm currently the President of the Machine Learning Club and (tentatively) Head of Construction for the FTC Robotics team, Apeiro. These roles have taught me how to guide a team, divide work effectively, and ensure everyone’s voice is heard. I explain difficult concepts using analogies, short demos, and step-by-step breakdowns, which can be especially helpful for hands-on topics like CAD or physics. If someone is struggling, I’ll approach them directly, explain the concept from a different angle, and walk through an example. Then I’ll give them a similar problem to try for themselves. I want every team member to feel supported, no matter their starting point.
column18: What I enjoy most about SciOly is the collaborative learning—you get to explore unique, real-world STEM topics that go beyond what’s taught in class. But when I joined Optics last year, I asked a question and got a dismissive answer: “Just use Snell’s Law.” I had to self-learn the entire topic just to compete. That showed me how this year’s weak leadership can discourage learning. I want to be the opposite—someone who builds up others and gives them the tools they need to succeed. I want members to not only learn new things but feel that they’re part of something meaningful.

column16:
To be honest, I hated biology as a class, but I loved biology. The difference? In class, I was constrained. On my own, I could explore freely and learned deeply. That’s the kind of learning I want to foster as Subject Director.

Let's face it; most members rely on self-studying, not EM lectures (they're just unfeasible for content-heavy events! Bio prioritizes breadth of knowledge). However, there were times that I myself felt lost when self studying. So, over the summer, I plan to create self-paced curriculums decked with videos both from YouTube and by me and my EMs, textbooks, and digital labs (like PhET simulations). I also want to play a role in streamlining the AI-powered SciOly website to guide learning. Academic meetings will then take on the role of answering questions and connecting concepts.

But I also want meetings to become more fun. Humans remember best when we are interested in what we learn, and so I propose mini "research competitions" where groups of members can delve deep into topics of their choosing to create biology-based games, lectures, or proposals etc based on certain prompts (like maybe  a game where other members can apply knowledge to treat patients) . After all, I found that my love for science came through the creativity that competitions like Exploravision and Genes in Space allowed me. Members can become experts in their areas and help teach others, promoting collaboration and teamwork. By making learning more fun, studying will become less of a chore and more of a passion, allowing us to push closer to Nats!!! I don't want competition preparation to be a hassle, I want it to be an intrinsic reward for learning.
column17:
Select Leadership Positions
Journal of Biology (JOB) Molecular Biology Senior Editor
-  Led groups of writers. Provide fact-checks and grammar checks over fun discussions about their articles. Also wrote and published 2 of my own articles (about Kombucha's Microbiology & Motor Proteins)

Game Theory and Economics Society (GTES) Co-President
- Expanded meetings from ~5 consistent members to ~15 by more advertising and making meetings more interactive. Demonstrated topics in Game Theory with a mixture of lecture and mini games. For example, at our last meeting I taught about the Game of Chicken by having members run at each other for a snack (seems funny, but really taught the topic well, and caused engagement to skyrocker, pulling members off of their phone and into club discussion).

As a teacher, my biggest goal is to promote understanding instead of sterile memorization. I hate it when facts/equations come out of nowhere. For example, when I play the role of a tutor in my OChem class, I always trace reaction mechanisms back to the why when I could easily just tell my friends about the reaction patterns.

I also make my learning as engaging and fun as I possibly can. I only teach what I myself am passionate about, so I want to make others interested as well. I try to incoorporate real-world applications when I talk about, say, evolution, and do my best to really show what I'm talking about through visuals and live demonstrations.
column18: I still remember the day of regionals. All the thrill, excitement, joy, and fear all cooped up into room 231. Yet, not even 1/3 of our team got to be there! This is especially sad when you realize meetings (as of now) are usually nothing like this, meaning most of the team never actually get to experience what makes SciOly so fun. I mean, these members are willingly giving up time that could be spent at home, or at a club, shouldn't we show them we care? Through my mini "research competitions," I seek to build SciOly as a big community where we help one another. With student-focused meetings, I hope to not only better prepare us for competitions by putting our members in charge of their learning, but also to help them use SciOly to explore fields outside of their school subjects; who knows? maybe by fostering a love for the Nervous system in A&P, someone may then want to pursue neurosurgery. Imagine the lasting impact SciOly could leave on someone's life by putting them in charge, giving them the opportunities, materials, and support they need to finally crack into their passions. I've made so many friends in SciOly and I want to see us succeed, but that can only come if we change our ways and take leaps we've never taken before.

column16: As the Chemistry and Inquiry Subject Director for Science Olympiads, I envision a transformative approach that ignites curiosity and cultivates excellence in young scientists. I will champion evidence-based frameworks, leveraging data-driven insights to refine competition formats and ensure they inspire critical thinking and innovation. Embracing inclusivity, I aim to implement outreach initiatives and mentorship programs to empower diverse participants, ensuring equitable access to opportunities that spark scientific passion. By incorporating cutting-edge technologies, such as virtual simulations and AI-driven analytical tools, I will enhance the learning experience while upholding ethical standards. Continuous learning will be a cornerstone, with professional development workshops for coaches and students to stay abreast of emerging chemical sciences trends. Finally, I will establish robust metrics to evaluate impact, transparently showcasing our successes to stakeholders and aligning with the Science Olympiads’ mission to cultivate the next generation of scientific leaders.

column16:
I want to change how we build binders by using AI for organization—not content generation. First, we’ll input our current binder into a model like Curser for broad reorganization and surface-level fact-checking. Then, EMs will go page by page, verifying and expanding each topic. To make this efficient, EMs will draft unfiltered “word vomit” notes organized by topic, then use AI to restructure them into a clean, readable format. This fixes our biggest issue: as binders grow, they become cluttered and hard to navigate, wasting time during competitions and costing points.

Over the summer, both EMs will review the entire binder so every section gets two sets of eyes for fact-checking and content improvement. If possible, they’ll go over it twice to ensure depth and accuracy. This rigorous review prepares EMs for their events and creates detailed binder to reach national level. To ensure consistent progress, EMs will create in depth weekly summer plans, which we’ll review during biweekly one-on-one meetings to stay accountable and productive.
column17: As the former Event Manager of Astronomy and Forensics and president of the writing club, I’ve spent the year creating and teaching three weekly lessons. This experience taught me how to teach effectively—by focusing on derivations and reasoning, not memorization. I always aim to explain equations or phenomena with qualitative logic so students can build on logic when tackling complex problems. When they forget a detail, they can reason their way back to it. I also encourage participation to reinforce new material by asking questions during the whole lesson about the material. In terms of interaction with students, I try to create a relaxed environment. I believe students learn best when they’re comfortable, so I make an effort to connect with them. I joke before or after class, chat with them, and try to always be friendly. In building that relationship I feel less like a stranger which allows students to learn better from me.
column18: I want to be Subject Director because I believe our team can be one of the best in the state, and I think this position would give me the ability to help make that happen. There are parts of SciOly that I think could be improved—things like how we study, how we run our events, and how we approach competitions—and being an SD gives me a place to voice those ideas and actually implement them. For example I want to help restructure how we teach and run lessons so they’re more engaging and effective, at least for my own events. I also want our team to compete more often, and having a say in how resources are allocated would help make that possible. As an SD, I can actually make meaningful changes that push both my events and the entire team forward.

column17:
As subject director, it’s their role to teach. But newsflash: most people suck at teaching. Just keep this in mind as you read along.
     During freshman year, I was in the leadership team of my SHSAT volunteering program. (Yes it’s SHSAT, but I put in a ton of effort regardless.) You may not know, but teaching kids is a literal nightmare. One half was forced to come, the other isn’t paying attention, and no one wants to be there.
     However, instead of begrudgingly teaching off the board, I taught lessons, with well, brainrot. And it f***ing worked. The entire class increased their scores by ~        80 points through word problems involving monkeys with 24g gyatts. 
     Because SciOly is more mature (right???), I won’t yap about skibidi rizz for the MOST PART. But recognize that I will be just as laidback, while still being a quality subject director for SciOly.
column18:
People who know me know that I am very passionate about the things I do. I drop anything that I don’t like and go above and beyond for my interests. Although I love SciOly as science competitions mixed with a fun time, I see its many flaws. It’s only natural for teams to have issues, but like my previous responses, I have a clear vision for changing SciOly for the better. Not only do I want my commitment to SciOly to shine, I want other people’s commitment to SciOly to come to fruition as well. 
     The only realistic way to create change is by getting primary/secondary leadership, so hey, here I am. Here to help, here to give ideas, here to do whatever is needed to improve this place. I’m here as a subject director candidate with actionable ideas to implement.
column19:
I’ll only count the commitment that involves time. 

1. 3 clubs. Machine Learning Clubs (Mon), Business Society (Wed), Math Club (Fri). I am treasurer of the Business Society. However, the other 2 shouldn’t interfere with anything because they’re on Monday and Friday. Commitment: 45 minute/week per club.
2. Research. This will only be an issue in the beginning of the school year, but I plan on having everything done by the end of the summer anyways. But I’ll still put it here. Commitment: 3-4 hrs/week in the beginning of the school year. 0-1 hr/week later in the year.

column15: As a two-year Science Olympiad competitor, I've demonstrated commitment through consistent performance and effective teamwork. My tournament experience includes regionals and other key competitions, where I've earned multiple medals which is proof of my ability to excel under pressure, deliver results, and the effort I put in. Beyond competitions, I have retained most of my knowledge from AP Biology concepts while learning more due to my ongoing microbiology research investigating bacterial responses to environmental factors. This work enhances my expertise in events like Disease Detectives and Designer Genes, as I regularly juggle around with similar topics.
column16: As a member for two years now, I feel like one issue that we always have had is our ability of not able to understand each members skill as a whole in any academic event. Instead of cramming test packet one or two weeks before regionals or states, I propose to taking short quizzes session during blocks once every 3 weeks or so for each academic event in order to act as a progress report to see where each member stands, thus allowing event managers and subject directors to help people who are in need. This improves the team as a whole, especially the people who are new to SciOly, giving them a direction to dig into, instead of only targeting individuals who are competing for the upcoming invitational or tournament.
column17: Though I haven’t held formal leadership roles, I have extensive experience teaching peers through study groups and SGI tutoring, adapting my style to each student’s needs—whether visual or discussion-based. I mainly prefer using analogies to explain complex topics and encourage questions being made. As a potential leader, I’d prioritize accessibility, offering support to any concerns that our members may have as I am always online whether it is email, messages, or in-person help. My flexibility ensures no student will be lost. While new to official SciOly leadership, my teaching experience and adaptive approach demonstrate my ability to mentor effectively. I aim to create an environment where members feel comfortable seeking help and promoting growth together.
column18: I want this position because I feel like SciOly is lacking in communications between the boards and their new members, especially the newer members who aren't really open to our community. This often leads to many questions that they might have to be lost. I value communication and believe that it can help SciOly to thrive and better prepare for next year's competition. I wanted to improve SciOly as I once was really lost and not knowing what to do before I joined. Being in this community helped me discovered my passion for Microbiology, so I wanted to hopefully inspire others like how I was inspired.
column19: I participate in a winter sport (~ 7 hours a week) and spring sport (~ 4 hours a week) that I am willing to drop if it overlaps with my SciOly leadership responsibilities. I am also a treasurer for Physics Club (~ 1-2 hour a week), but that happens on Friday, so I believe there would be no overlaps at all.
